speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE], 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. OK, camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon, when we finished the last tape, you were telling us how you were questioned by people in the first school and how you went to the second school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the teachers looking after you that came from France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. and Mrs. Dybnis, somehow we never saw them again. Because we were split.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you concerned about what had happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because we didn't know where they were, of course. And so we mixed with, with-- not only with children, but elderly people which we didn't know. So it was like a-- how can I say? It was no longer a-- a children's home. It was a transit-- transit camp. Now, in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], when we arrived in the [INAUDIBLE], we were allowed to go out once a week. But we were only allowed to go out with-- with a Swiss Guard walking. So they used to take us maybe two or three hours on an excursion in the field, or somewhere like that. And that's about it, you know, just to get our--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, we just-- as kids, you know, we made new-- new friends, of course, and we-- nothing much. We just stayed there. And that's it. We were watching people behind the-- being a playground, a big playground, it-- it-- it was not barbed wire but it was mesh wires around, so that we were not allowed. And every now and again, we'd see that the Swiss Guards going and going around and around all the time. And I remember that I was-- it was-- Rosh Hashanah came. And we were talking about-- we were talking about we didn't have enough [NON-ENGLISH]. So somehow the elderly people got together and asked the Swiss Guard whether they could allow them to go outside and get some-- some religious thing, [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH] and-- and tallis, which they did allow. The only thing we didn't have is a shofar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the story goes at that, I was listening to what was going on. And I went outside in the playground and I-- the next day. And I suddenly see a gentleman is passing by with a [NON-ENGLISH]. Obviously, it was a [INAUDIBLE]. I recognized he was Jewish, you see. I called him over. And I said to him in Yiddish, is there a possibility that you could get us a shofar? So he said to me in Yiddish, stay here. I'll be back very soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed and I stayed. And he finally came. And we had to watch so that we were not being caught by the guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he slipped underneath a bag. And inside was a shofar. So I took it in inside into the room where we had the service. I became a hero. [LAUGHS] Where did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, I told them the story, they couldn't believe it. And I in the-- I was 10. So you know, I became a very popular-- popular fellow. In French, we call it [SPEAKING FRENCH]. [SPEAKING FRENCH] means--

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know where the word in English is. Somebody who knows how to get things, you see? So it was this. And then we were moved into another section. Excuse me, but were the people very [INAUDIBLE], very religious that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I would say. Well, I don't know all, but the majority were. You know, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur was something. I mean, after all, they were saved, you know. And so they were thankful to the-- to do the davening. And us, coming from a-- from a religious home, naturally to us meant a lot as well, you see. So we were strict about that. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask you, please. The-- the food that you had there, was that-- that couldn't have been kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was not-- I don't remember, but it was not food that we were not allowed to eat. They didn't give us anything like treif stuff. Excuse me. It must have been-- how would you mention, vegetable? You know, that was-- because I can't remember that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you conscious of having more food once you were in Switzerland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They gave-- the food was all right there. We were well-looked-after, as far as that was concerned. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you this? The irony that in France, you lived freely, and yet you were not free people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Switzerland, you were free people. And yet, you were guarded.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we were guarded. But at the same time, we were happy to have a roof over our head, you see. And then afterwards, when we moved-- we moved into a better place. Again, it became full, the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they moved us into Montreux. Montreaux was-- it's about two or three hours from Geneva. So they took us on a train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was called, in Montreaux, Belmont Hotel it was. The hotel was turned into a camp, something beautiful, and overlooking France on the Lake of Geneva. It was absolutely beautiful. And there, we had more freedom, you see. So we were allowed to go out, but, again, with a-- with a Swiss Guard. But it was not as guarded as it was the other place. Somehow maybe the Swiss trusted us. They knew that we wouldn't run away, because-- so anyway, we stayed there for a few months. And suddenly, we were notified that [INAUDIBLE] had managed to open up a children's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you a little bit about the conditions and the living in Montreaux?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Montreaux, it was very good. It was very good, because to us, it was luxury. And as I said, we could see across the Lake Evian, which, you know the water comes from. We could see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, what we saw is, you know, the Allied bombing the-- not Allied, but the-- the German bombing, the place across the-- you know? We could-- we could watch this. I mean, we were not happy about. And they used to say, please come in, in case, you know, they come over there, over here. But there's no schooling at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no schooling, no, no schooling at all. And we were, as I said, we enjoyed going into-- into excursions, you know, for the day, walking. And it was quite good.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the mountains, yes. Yes, it was all mountainous. And then we were notified after a few months that a children's home was going to open up in-- in a German-Swiss part not far from St. Gall. And the place was called Wattwil. My sister, then we split. My sister was assigning to a au pair in-- in a family in Zurich. And there, she was accepted, because she was also very orthodox, into a very orthodox family. And she lived there. And obviously, she must have helped and so on. And my brother and I went into this home in Wattwil. And what happened is this. Every day, there were more children coming in all the time. And there, we went to school. But we had-- we learned Switzerdeutsch. Excuse me. [DRINKS] And, of course, we-- the food was good there. We enjoyed being there. And then-- and then the beauty of that place is six months a year, it was under snow. So to go to school, they had given us either skates, or skis, or luge, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon. What is a luge?

speaker: interviewee
text: A luge is a-- what do they call it, this thing that you sit on it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A toboggan?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a toboggan. You sit on it, and you go down the hill with it. So now, remember, I always was anxious to get a pair of skis. And in the home where we were, it was like a pension. Because part of the home was a-- was a people used to come and eat, like a restaurant in Wattwil. And the owner's son was a ski champion. So he used to teach us how to ski. So this was good. And we used to go to school like that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Swiss Jews who were looking after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I can't remember. But, you know, it was quite pleasant. We were safe. We were in the-- in the-- in the country amongst animals and so on, you know. And the people were actually quite nice. And so I can't really complain on that. And we grew up like this for-- for about nearly a year. And it became too small, so we moved into another home in Spiez. And there, we were I think about 60 boys and girls again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any from the original group in France with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a few, yes, who joined us again. And then we managed to-- Miriam and Henri Dybnis joined us as well. They were in charge of us, which we were very, very happy. Because to us, they were like parents. And so they looked after us all along till after the-- till actually, till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was also an old, a big old house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very old, yes, a very big-- it was an older home. I don't remember what it was before. But it had three or four story high. And it was-- it was built in wood. So because we were so many kids and they could get up to mischief, so they decided to get fire brigade to do exercise every three or four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Spiez, in Switzerland, in Spiez, in the German part of Switzerland and, actually, not far from the Lake Constance. You could actually see from where we were, Germany across the lake. And we could see the Allies actually bombing the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? And it was fascinating. And of course, we see this plane coming over. Anyway, so we used to do exercise, because in case somebody set fire to the home. So it was important that they teach us how to look after ourselves. So they used to have drills. We used to go up on the second floor. And they used to make us jump into these canvas, you know. [LAUGHS] They used to teach us how to look after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so there, we went to school again in the village. And I can't remember the incident at all, except again, we were keen skiers. And that was the fun of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the relationship with the village children was good?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were good, because they used to bring us, you know, chocolate. And, of course, chocolate had to be on coupons. You know, you had to have special coupons to obtain that. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the coupons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the home, the children's home was-- we didn't get personally, we didn't. But the-- the children's home was-- was allowed to have, by the Swiss government, so much coupons, so that we were allowed to buy chocolate and clothing, of course, and other things. And the people from the village used to come and give us clothes, or cakes, and things. And I will never forget. I was going to school once. And I found on the road a wallet. So I opened it up. And I saw there was-- it was full of coupons. And being honest as I am, [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] on me, I decided to take the wallet to the police station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And two days later, we were sitting in the dining room in the home. And this couple came in with the police, and so on, and speaking to Mr. Dybnis, our director. We want to see a gentleman by the name of Leon. And I was over there. They pointed that. Come over here. And they came in with a big cake, chocolate cake. They gave me that. And they said, we want to thank you very much for being honest. So you see, it paid dividends. And, of course, they had started to invite me on Sundays. They lived not far from the home. And they said, please come-- come to eat at our place on Sunday. And of course, I had to have the permission to. And they gave me a pair of skis, which was very nice. [LAUGHS] And so, you know, of course, in the home, whenever we had something, we had to share with everybody. Because, you know, chocolates, we hadn't seen chocolate since before the war. So it was really fantastic to have that. And because of that, the-- the the other-- what are called-- the population around, you know, being a small village, must have heard. And they became more and more friendly. So they give us things, you know. They send fruit and the things. You know, it was good. And we stayed till 1945. And in 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: For two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, for two years. It was 19-- yeah, still the war finished. Early-- and then the Swiss started to tell all the refugee, please get out. Go back to your country. So with-- with Miriam and Henri Dybnis, we all closed the home, took a train to back to France. And I was hoping when we got to Paris that somebody of my family would wait on the station. Because when we arrived at the station, other kids had their-- some of their family, cousins, or aunties, or whatever. And I was really disappointed that nobody turned up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Through the war, you had lost touch with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost touch with my parents, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us that Raymonde had taken you to--

speaker: interviewee
text: She told us they were taken to-- to Auschwitz to Drancy. But we didn't know from Drancy where. We thought they were in a camp outside Paris called Drancy.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we didn't realize they were killed. So we thought-- you know, I thought, maybe my sister must have thought different. But I, being-- how old was I? I was 12 years old by then. And my brother was 13. So when we arrived in Paris, naturally, I was a bit disappointed not to see anyone in the station. And of course, I was in tears, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got into this home from [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] means [NON-ENGLISH]. It's like the Jewish Welfare of Society in Australia. And we were put into this home in Noye. Noye is just on the outskirt of Paris. And it's part of the suburb of Paris, of course. And we stayed there for a few months. But a few-- my sister didn't come with us. She stayed in Switzerland with these people in-- in Zurich, by the name of Mr. Bolag, and-- which I will tell you a story, you know, very soon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother and I, we got back to France. On a-- on a Friday, one Friday, we decided to go to-- I wanted to find out where I lived. So us jumped over the fence, didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had some pocket money. I don't-- we were given pocket money, so I had enough for fare to go with the metro. And I was 12. My brother was 13. And I remember the station where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we took the metro. And, of course, I had to be very careful. Because in France, we were told that when kids during the daytime are running in the streets or in the metro, they can be arrested and say, why aren't you at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is a good thing, thinking back. So I had to try and avoid the police, you see. Because, you know, I was scared to be arrested or something like that. We finally got to where I lived. And I remember very well. You know, things sticks in your mind. And when I got to the place where we lived in [INAUDIBLE]. And when we opened the door, the concierge came in. She got the shock of her life and didn't know that we were alive. So I began to question her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think she was pleased to see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think that they were pleased to see us. In fact, I think she got the shock of her life. And as I said before, at the age of 12, I was like a grown up of 18 to 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was always suspicious, because of my circumstances. So I began to question her. And I said to her, who lives in our apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she told me, Algerians. In those days, there was a lot of influx from the colonies, of course. And that quarter, the Jewish quarter, became virtually taken over by the Algerians, and because many Jews didn't return.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what about our belonging? And she said to me, well, maybe there's some of them there, you know. And I could see that she had jewelry on her fingers. And my mother-- my sister had told me that my mother had some jewelry and so on. So I said to her, I presume that part of the jewelery that you're wearing, as small as I was, belongs to my parents. So she got a little bit suspicious about that. And she said to me, hang on a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: She comes out with a very big frame of my-- my parents' wedding photo. So always in a frame, it was. I was so pleased about that. And-- and I said to her, please tell me about what actually happened to my-- how-- how they were caught. And I said to her, I presume you notified the authority there were Jews in these-- into these units. And she didn't like that. But she said to me and my brother, come inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went inside. And she said, that was-- that belonged to your mother. It was a sewing machine. So I pulled the drawer open.

speaker: interviewee
text: And inside, I saw a photograph of my father. And there was a watch, my mother's watch. So naturally, I took that, the photograph of my father, a tiny little photograph, folded it in half, which I have. I will show you. And you see, then I-- I-- it was getting late. And since I didn't travel on Shabbat, and I decided to-- I wanted to find out. My uncle lived not very far from where we lived. And I didn't know whether he was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to my brother, let's go and-- let's go and see whether Uncle Maurice is alive before we take back the metro to go back into the home. It was getting very close to Shabbat. So we left her. We left her. And we told her we'd come back sometime. And I took the photograph under my arm, of course. And we walked to where my uncle used to live in [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, again, asked the concierge, is Mr. Czibulski live there? She said, yes. Is he-- and I wasn't quite sure. Because I began to ask a question. Because I thought, Czibulski maybe is a common name. So I said, has he got a son by the name of Henri? And she said, yes. And his wife is Susanne. [INAUDIBLE] Susanne? They said, yes. They said, they're not here now. They're at work. So I knew they were alive. They didn't know that we were alive. So what happened, we took the metro back. And after Shabbat on Sunday morning, we decided to do the same thing-- up over the fence, took the metro again, knocked on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: To their amazement, they saw us. And, of course, we fell in-- into each other's arms and we're tears. And when they saw us, my Auntie Susanne said, I'll go down, and we'll have breakfast. You're right. I got tears. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So as she-- as she went down, two minutes later, she comes back empty handed. And I said, that was quick. She says not [INAUDIBLE] Susanne. I'm her twin sister, never met before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you know, I couldn't believe that was a twin sister. I thought, that was quick. She went down and took two minutes, but she didn't have anything under her arm. And she said, no, I'm her twin sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- you know we decided to-- so my auntie came back with bread, baguette, and all that sort of thing. And so we-- we had breakfast. And of course, we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decided to tell each other stories, what happened to us, what happened to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me-- I remember before the war, he came to say goodbye to my mother and to us. And he joined the-- the French army. And his story was that he was caught with his-- with his-- unit. He was caught by the German and sent to camp in Germany for two years. And being a French soldier, luckily, they didn't touch him. He was prisoner of war in Germany. And he came back after the war. And he didn't know where we were, whatever. And so that was a most amazing story. And he said to me, I'd like you to meet-- there is a wedding tonight, one of your cousins. So he took us, first of all, to-- we walked down [INAUDIBLE] to Boulevard [INAUDIBLE]. And there, there was a cafe. And he introduced me to the owner of the cafe. That was my-- another uncle. It was my-- my father had a sister. And that was her husband. And, of course, we set in the cafe. And we talked and so on, you know. And then he took us to another uncle, to another cousin, which we-- of course, today, they must be passed away. And that evening, we went to the wedding. We were the life of the party, of course, my brother and I. They couldn't believe. And it was a fantastic reunion, of course. They couldn't look after us, because they didn't have the means. So we had to go back to the home. But we saw every now and again. Then we moved away from the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you a little bit about the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like there? You went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school in Noye, yes. But I don't recollect too much of it, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you, your brother was 13 when you left Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened with bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother became bar mitzvah in the home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Switzerland, that's right, in Spiez. He became [INAUDIBLE]. Yes, he became bar mitzvah there. Myself, well, I was 12. So I was not bar mitzvah then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do something special for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the home did. You know, they had what you call, a bit-- a bit more food than-- you know, whenever somebody had a bar mitzvah, that made things. Yes, they made fuss about it. You know, we had cake and all that sort of thing. By the way, in Spiez, coming back, every Friday, we had to do our own cooking. So, you know, Switzerland is known for muesli. So that was always every Friday, we had muesli. And we were also, we were cooking apple tart. That was our speciality. So we learned how to [INAUDIBLE] apple tart. [LAUGHS] It was good. Because even today, I always like that. Anyway, so coming back to-- to-- to Noye, we left Noye. And we were sent to then in Lyon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason for going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was too small. And they wanted a home. That particular home, they wanted to keep for people who-- for family who couldn't look after their children. They were destitute. They couldn't look after their children. They were-- some of the parents were still alive. So they moved us in Lyon. In Lyon, we again, we-- we met some of the friends, not all of them. But the Dybnis' had disappeared from our life. We didn't know what happened to them. And in Lyon, we had a few incidents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had-- we were given some-- the-- the I believe that the-- that Americans joined committee. It was like the [INAUDIBLE]. Sent a lot of the clothes, but they didn't distribute them to us. And somehow, I found out-- I found out that the clothes were stuck in the attic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I sneaked in. And I found all those clothes, pile of clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about that on the next tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think that this tape is just coming to an end now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope is not too--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape three of the interview with Leon Wise. It's the 5th of November, 1995. And we are in Sydney, Australia.
